title: Quiz: Channel Capacity
description: Test your understanding of channel capacity and Shannon's coding theorem

questions.question: The capacity of a binary symmetric channel with crossover probability p = 0 is:
questions.explanation: When p = 0, there are no errors, so C = 1 - H(0) = 1 - 0 = 1 bit. The channel perfectly transmits each bit.

questions.question: For a binary symmetric channel with p = 0.5, the capacity is:
questions.explanation: When p = 0.5, the output is independent of the input (random coin flip). C = 1 - H(0.5) = 1 - 1 = 0 bits. The channel is useless.

questions.question: According to Shannon's channel coding theorem, if the rate R > C:

options.text: Error probability can still be made arbitrarily small
Error probability is bounded away from zero
The code length must be infinite

questions.explanation: The converse of Shannon's theorem states that for R > C, the error probability cannot be made arbitrarily small - reliable communication is impossible above capacity.

questions.question: Noiseless feedback increases the capacity of a discrete memoryless channel.
questions.explanation: False. Shannon proved that feedback does not increase capacity for DMCs. However, feedback can simplify encoding and reduce decoding delay.

questions.question: The binary erasure channel with erasure probability 0.3 has capacity:
questions.explanation: BEC capacity is simply C = 1 - ε = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7 bits. Erasures are 'known unknowns', making capacity linear in (1-ε).

questions.question: Channel capacity C = max_{p(x)} I(X;Y) is maximized over:
questions.explanation: Capacity is found by maximizing mutual information over all possible input distributions p(x), while the channel p(y|x) is fixed.

questions.question: The BEC always has higher capacity than the BSC for the same error parameter.
questions.explanation: True. For the same p, C_BEC = 1-p ≥ 1-H(p) = C_BSC. Equality only at p=0. Erasures are easier to handle than unknown errors.

questions.question: Which statements about capacity-achieving codes are correct? (Select all)

options.text: Random codes are practical to implement
Block length must approach infinity for zero error

questions.explanation: a) Random codes achieve capacity but are impractical. b) False: random codes need exponential storage. c) LDPC, Turbo, and Polar codes can approach capacity. d) Zero error requires n → ∞.
